anomalies.description: Checkout failures coincided with payment-provider TLS handshake rejections; the normally logged 23:05 TLS renewal job has no scheduler entry, and the payment service presented a certificate that had expired at 23:05 until the retry sweep installed a valid replacement.
The deploy was rolled back at 23:25, but the error rate remained unchanged until 23:40, contrary to the declared behavior of a deploy-caused failure.
The database-overload hypothesis conflicts with database latency remaining 22-35ms, below the declared 50ms normal limit, during the incident.

evidence.content: Deploy completed 23:00. Rollback completed 23:25. Error rate unchanged by rollback; errors ceased 23:40.
evidence.label: Deploy and rollback log

evidence.content: Database latency stable at 22-35ms throughout the incident window.
evidence.label: Database latency metrics

evidence.content: Payment API rejecting connections 23:10-23:40 with TLS handshake failures. Connections resume 23:40.
evidence.label: Payment provider log

evidence.content: Entries present for 22:00 and 00:00 jobs. No entry for the 23:05 TLS renewal job tonight. Retry sweep runs at 23:38.
evidence.label: Cron scheduler log

evidence.content: Audit of certificates presented by the payment service 23:05-23:40: the certificate presented from 23:10 to 23:37 had expired at 23:05. The 23:38 retry sweep installed a valid replacement certificate at 23:38:41; the first successful payment-provider handshake followed at 23:39:52. No provider-side incident is recorded for this window.
evidence.label: Certificate audit

case.expected_behavior: Deploys either cause immediate errors or none; a rollback removes any deploy-caused failure within minutes. The nightly TLS certificate renewal cron for the payment service runs at 23:05 and always writes a log line confirming renewal. Payment API calls succeed with valid certificates. Database latency stays under 50ms at that traffic level.
case.observed_outcome: E-commerce checkout returned a spike of HTTP 500 errors from 23:10 to 23:40. The on-call team blamed the 23:00 deploy and rolled it back at 23:25, but errors continued until 23:40 and then stopped on their own.

coherence.explanation: H3 explains the absent 23:05 renewal confirmation, the expired certificate presented during the TLS-failure period, the valid replacement installed by the 23:38 retry sweep, and the first successful handshake after that replacement. It also explains why rollback did not affect errors and is compatible with normal database latency. The score is not 100 because the supplied evidence does not establish why the 23:05 renewal job lacked an entry or whether the 23:00 deploy contributed to that renewal failure.

expected_absent.description: The 23:05 TLS renewal job's confirmation log line, which the declared normal behavior says is always written, is absent from the scheduler log.
expected_absent.significance: This observable absence is anchored to the declared renewal behavior. Together with E5's expired certificate, it supports a failed or unexecuted local renewal path.

expected_absent.description: Error recovery within minutes of the 23:25 rollback is absent; the error rate remained unchanged until 23:40.
expected_absent.significance: A deploy-caused failure should have cleared shortly after rollback under the declared behavior. The missing recovery materially weakens a deploy-only explanation.

expected_present.description: Database latency remained below the declared 50ms normal threshold, at 22-35ms throughout the incident window.
expected_present.significance: This normal observation directly conflicts with the prediction that database overload caused the checkout errors.

unexpected_absent.description: The elevated database-latency episode that database overload should have produced is absent from the incident metrics.
unexpected_absent.significance: Latency above the declared normal threshold is a predicted effect of H2; its absence rejects the database-overload explanation.

unexpected_absent.description: A provider-side incident record is absent for the TLS-failure window.
unexpected_absent.significance: H4 predicts an independent provider-side TLS incident, but E5 explicitly records none while also documenting a local certificate correction before recovery.

unexpected_present.description: Checkout returned a spike of HTTP 500 errors from 23:10 to 23:40.
unexpected_present.significance: This is the outcome requiring explanation; E3 records payment TLS handshake failures in the same incident window.

unexpected_present.description: The payment provider rejected connections with TLS handshake failures from 23:10 to 23:40.
unexpected_present.significance: This technical symptom identifies the payment TLS path as the mechanism coinciding with checkout failures.

unexpected_present.description: The payment service presented a certificate expired at 23:05 from 23:10 to 23:37; the retry sweep installed a valid replacement at 23:38:41, followed by the first successful provider handshake at 23:39:52.
unexpected_present.significance: This directly connects an unusable local certificate state to the failed-handshake interval and connects local certificate replacement to recovery.

hypotheses.statement: The 23:05 payment-service TLS renewal did not successfully provide a usable certificate, leaving the service to present an expired certificate and fail payment-provider TLS handshakes until the 23:38 retry sweep installed a valid replacement.

supported_by.reason: E4 supports H3 because the normally logged 23:05 renewal entry is absent while adjacent scheduler entries are present, and E4 records the 23:38 retry sweep.
E3 supports H3 because TLS handshake failures occupy the checkout-error window and connections resume at its end.
E1 supports H3 because rollback did not alter the error rate, consistent with a failure in payment-service certificate state rather than deployed checkout behavior alone.

E5 strongly supports H3 because it records an expired certificate during the TLS-failure interval, installation of a valid replacement by the retry sweep, and the first successful provider handshake after that installation.

hypotheses.would_be_refuted_by: Payment-service renewal execution records and certificate-selection diagnostics showing that a valid, unexpired certificate was presented for the failed 23:10-23:37 handshakes and that the 23:38 replacement was not the certificate used when recovery began.

contradicted_by.reason: E4 weakens H1 because the absent expected 23:05 renewal entry identifies a local renewal-path failure not explained merely by checkout deploy timing.
E1 weakens H1 because rollback completed at 23:25 but errors remained unchanged until 23:40; a deploy-caused failure should have cleared within minutes under the declared behavior.
E5 weakens H1 because it supplies a direct local certificate mechanism for the failure and recovery, rather than evidence that deployed checkout code itself failed.

hypotheses.statement: The 23:00 deploy broke the checkout flow.

supported_by.reason: E1 makes H1 a candidate only because deployment preceded the failure window. Timing alone does not establish causation, and rollback behavior contradicts a deploy-only account.

hypotheses.would_be_refuted_by: The 23:00 deployment change set and payment-service scheduler configuration showing no change capable of disabling, misconfiguring, or preventing the 23:05 TLS renewal job.

contradicted_by.reason: E4 weakens H4 because an independent provider TLS incident does not explain the absent local renewal confirmation.

E5 kills H4 as stated because it records no provider-side incident, an expired local certificate during failed handshakes, and local valid-certificate installation before the first successful handshake.

hypotheses.resurrection_condition: Revive only if provider diagnostics for 23:10-23:40 document a provider-side TLS fault that independently caused the handshake rejections despite the payment service's local certificate replacement.
hypotheses.statement: An independent payment-provider TLS-side incident caused the handshake rejections; the missing 23:05 renewal log is unrelated.

supported_by.reason: E3 originally made H4 plausible because it records provider-side connection rejection, but it did not establish that the provider was at fault.

hypotheses.would_be_refuted_by: Provider incident records or connection diagnostics showing no provider-side TLS fault while the payment service's certificate changed from expired to valid before successful handshakes resumed.

contradicted_by.reason: E2 kills H2 as stated: database latency remained 22-35ms throughout the window, below the declared under-50ms normal behavior, rather than showing the elevated latency predicted by overload.
E3 further weakens H2 because it records TLS handshake failures during the precise checkout-error window.
E5 further contradicts H2 by documenting an expired local certificate during failure and a valid replacement before handshake recovery.

hypotheses.resurrection_condition: Revive only if corrected database telemetry for 23:10-23:40 shows latency above 50ms or a database saturation event causally producing the checkout 500 responses.
hypotheses.statement: Database overload under evening traffic caused the checkout errors.
hypotheses.would_be_refuted_by: Database telemetry covering the affected checkout requests that shows latency remained below 50ms and no saturation throughout 23:10-23:40.

learning.summary: E5 resolves the prior central uncertainty. H3 gains because E5 directly records the expired certificate throughout the failed-handshake period and a valid replacement installed by the retry sweep before recovery. H4 is rejected because E5 records no provider-side incident and supplies a local certificate-and-recovery mechanism. H1 loses further confidence because the direct certificate mechanism and rollback ineffectiveness outweigh deploy timing, although a deploy contribution to the renewal failure remains untested. H2 remains rejected; E5 adds a competing direct mechanism but E2 remains its killing evidence.

updates.reason: E5 directly confirms the predicted unusable certificate state during the TLS failures and confirms valid certificate installation by the 23:38 retry sweep before the first successful handshake.
E5 provides a direct certificate failure-and-recovery sequence, while E1 still shows rollback did not produce the recovery H1 predicts. Deploy timing alone remains insufficient.
E5 records no provider-side incident and instead documents an expired local certificate followed by locally installed valid replacement before recovery; this kills H4 as stated.
No change in lifecycle: E2 still directly rejects the required latency prediction. E5 supplies an additional non-database mechanism but does not alter the decisive latency evidence.

mirror_question: If H3 is false, the clue likely being ignored is whether the 23:00 deploy altered the payment-service renewal scheduler or its configuration: E5 proves the expired-certificate mechanism, but not why the 23:05 renewal path failed.

missing_evidence.description: The 23:00 deployment change set and payment-service scheduler or renewal configuration before deployment, after deployment, and after rollback. This would determine whether the deploy changed the 23:05 renewal path that E4 and E5 show failed to leave a usable certificate.
missing_evidence.impact_if_found: If the deploy directly disabled or misconfigured the renewal path, H1 would rise as an upstream cause of H3's certificate failure. If no deploy change affected renewal scheduling, certificate selection, or renewal configuration, H1 would fall further and H3 would remain the standalone explanation.

next_test.description: Compare the 23:00 deployment change set with payment-service TLS renewal scheduler and certificate configuration immediately before deployment, after deployment, and after rollback to determine whether the deploy altered the 23:05 renewal path.

outcome_map.observed_result: The deploy changed or disabled the renewal scheduler, renewal configuration, certificate path, or permissions in a manner that prevented the 23:05 renewal from providing a usable certificate.
No deployed change affected renewal scheduling, certificate selection, renewal configuration, or permissions, while the renewal path independently failed at 23:05.
The deployment diff or relevant configuration history is unavailable for the interval.

open_case_index.explanation: The previously central anomalies are substantially explained: X4 and X7 establish the failed local renewal and expired certificate, X3 establishes the matching TLS failures, and E5 establishes valid replacement before successful recovery. The remaining open issue is upstream causation: no supplied evidence determines whether the renewal-path failure was independent or introduced by the 23:00 deploy. Database overload and an independent provider incident are rejected by E2 and E5.

prime_suspect.absolving_datum: Payment-service renewal execution and certificate-selection diagnostics showing that an unexpired valid certificate was presented during the 23:10-23:37 failed handshakes, or that the 23:38 replacement did not account for the subsequent successful handshake.
prime_suspect.condemning_datum: Renewal execution diagnostics showing that the 23:05 job failed or did not execute, leaving the expired certificate selected until the 23:38 retry installed the valid replacement.
prime_suspect.justification: H3 explains A1 through the absent renewal confirmation, expired certificate, TLS failures, valid retry-sweep replacement, and subsequent successful handshake. It also explains A2's rollback ineffectiveness and remains compatible with A3's normal database latency. H1 does not explain why rollback failed to reduce errors and has only temporal support; H4 is contradicted by E5's absence of a provider incident. H3 remains limited by the unobserved reason the 23:05 renewal path failed.
